verdict_reasoning: STIM1 is a type 1 single-pass transmembrane protein that resides primarily in the ER membrane, where it senses luminal Ca2+ depletion via its EF-hand domain. Upon store depletion it oligomerizes and translocates to ER-PM junctions where it directly gates Orai1/CRAC channels on the plasma membrane. During this process the STIM1 C-terminal cytoplasmic domain reaches into and clusters at ER-PM junctions, but its single TM domain remains ER-resident; its N-terminus is ER-luminal. However, a pool of STIM1 has been detected at the plasma membrane itself (N-terminus extracellular) in certain cell types and particularly upon activation/store depletion — surface biotinylation and flow cytometry studies confirm a PM-resident fraction. This activation-induced PM pool constitutes documented surface accessibility of the ectodomain, qualifying it as contextual/cell_state_induced rather than a constitutive surface receptor. The dominant compartment is ER, but the activation-induced PM minority pool is real and has been exploited for surface-directed approaches.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and stability of the plasma-membrane STIM1 pool is debated; some studies see constitutive low-level PM display while others see it only upon activation or in specific cell types — unclear if this warrants 'yes' vs 'contextual'.
